data.title: Newsmax: Valuation Is Normalized, But Not Yet Attractive
data.description:
There is an argument that the insane early trading in Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX) makes it more likely that the stock will become an attractive buy in the future. After its initial public offering in March closed at $10 per share, NMAX stock within two sessions cleared $200 — a crazily parabolic run even by the standards of a bull market that has a few notable movers.

Indeed, as a fellow author pointed out, at the brief peak, Newsmax was more valuable than its rival, conservative giant Fox Corporation (FOX) (FOXA). Its second-day rise was literally historic. The bubble quickly popped, however; at Wednesday's close, Newsmax shares are not terribly far above their IPO price.

That crazy bubble, however, does create at least a hypothetical scenario in which Newsmax stock winds up falling too far. The quick collapse knocked out any traders looking for a meme and/or political play; it also perhaps gave traditional investors the first impression of a stock not even worth bothering with. Indeed, with NMAX heading back to the low double-digits, there is at least the outline of a fundamental bull case here.

Unfortunately, for now it's just an outline, as management, growth, and secular concerns still dominate. But there are scenarios where NMAX stock does prove to be a decent long-term play at this price — and even a potential buy if shares keep dropping.

NMAX Stock Is Better Than It Looks

Admittedly, at first look, Newsmax still looks pretty badly overvalued. Trailing twelve-month Adjusted EBITDA is just $1.7 million, and that figure reverted to a $3.4 million loss in the first half of 2025 following $5.1 million in profit on that basis the year before. EV/revenue, based on 2025 guidance and accounting for settlement liabilities (the most recent relating to litigation by Dominion Voting Systems for defamation in Newsmax's coverage of the 2020 election), is in the range of 7.5x (EV is ~$1.4 billion, and Newsmax is guiding for $180-$190 million in revenue this year). Major media companies including Fox, Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY) are in the 1x-2x range, and those seem obviously better businesses.

But as I wrote not long after the IPO, there are ways for NMAX to close that gap to peers. Growth from advertising, digital, and particularly affiliate fees all suggest Newsmax's top-line performance should be much better than that of other media companies. And the nature of the operating model means that incremental revenue should have exceptionally high profit margins; it doesn't cost that much more to produce a show with three million viewers than it does a show with one million.

In other words, despite the headline multiples NMAX no longer has a 'meme stock' valuation. And on that front, there does seem to be a bit of good news relative to affiliate fees. Around the IPO, the Financial Times estimated that Newsmax's affiliate fees were $0.10-$0.20 per subscriber per month; it appears that the actual numbers may be even lower. In late August, Newsmax issued a press release highlighting an analyst rating from Noble Capital, which cited estimates that affiliate fees are actually under $0.10 at the moment.

To be clear, it's not guaranteed that Newsmax's press release means those estimates are correct. One would think that Newsmax would be careful not to issue incorrect information under its own name, but it's also worth noting that the press release itself — which basically rewrites a bullish analyst report — is exceptionally unusual. (I don't ever recall seeing a publicly traded company issue a press release about analyst coverage, and certainly not one as detailed as this.) But as the report (and estimates from industry source SNL Kagan) points out, affiliate fees elsewhere range from $0.40 up to ~$2 for Fox News.

With a current 40 million subscribers, and room for likely another ~20 million more (Fox News had 61 million at the end of June, per Nielsen estimates cited in its 10-K), affiliate fees alone can probably get Newsmax stock into the range of reasonable valuation. An incremental $0.25 on the existing base adds ~$120 million; getting another 10 million subs another $30 million. That $150 million in incremental revenue could turn into ~$100 million in EBITDA, at which point NMAX is trading at a more reasonable 13-14x. (This is a version of the core case Noble made in its report; it valued the stock at $23, a clean double at this point.)

Add in growing ad revenue and perhaps some optionality from an Elon Musk/Twitter-style buyout (in which a deep-pocketed buyer values the distribution more than the profits) and it's not hard to build a fairly reasonable fundamental case at this point.

Good News And Bad News From 1H Earnings

And again, given the quick implosion of the post-IPO bubble, one might think that the bull case here is largely hidden. That case is not obvious on its face, and the fact that the stock touched $230 should suggest that many investors aren't even looking that hard. A fairly relentless decline of late — NMAX has hit an all-time low as I write this, and is headed for its seventh red day in eight sessions — adds to the sense that valuation probably has come in line.

And those declines come after a pair of quarterly earnings report that net/net look modestly positive for the business and for the stock. The results don't suggest much improvement on affiliate fees yet: that revenue stream is up 10% year-over-year in the first half, but that's in large part because the 1H 2024 revenue base appears to have been relatively small. (Newsmax didn't earn any affiliate fees at all until last year.) Growth slowed to 7.3% in Q2, and likely comes in even lighter in the second half as year-prior comparisons normalize. To be fair, the affiliate fee tailwind can't be a short-term boost, as those contracts are typically negotiated on a multi-year basis.

But in advertising, higher ratings and perhaps better customers are driving material growth. Advertising revenue grew nearly 20% year-over-year in the first half, even with the digital channel showing a decline in an off-year in the election cycle. (Those cycles are material even for local channel operators like Gray Media (GTN); the impact on a news/politics channel is likely even larger.) Incremental margins on that revenue stream probably aren't quite as high (bigger shows mean higher paychecks for on-air talent, producers, etc., and likely require better production values) but they're still attractive, and adding ~$20 million in revenue on an annual run-rate basis seems to position Newsmax going forward. The fact that ratings are increasing in the year after an election too seems to support the case that NMAX is a rare growth story in the media space.

The concern, however, is on the cost side. Cost of revenue — basically production costs, along with spending on third-party platforms for customer advertising campaigns — jumped 23.6% in the first half, dramatically outpacing the 14.9% increase in overall revenue. Some of this spending is an investment in Newsmax 2, which the company is developing as an ad-supported streaming channel, but even so the spike is a concern. (It's a reminder of how small the business is relative to media behemoths that the 10-Q calls out the funeral of Pope Francis and the conclave that elected Pope Leo XIV as material contributors to a year-over-year decline in Broadcast Segment Adjusted EBITDA. By the standards of major news organizations, neither event would seem to be particularly unusual in terms of expense.)

In essence, the first half both supports and undercuts the bull case. The argument that Newsmax is growing quickly in what is probably the most attractive (only attractive?) category in linear television gets some help from the strong ratings and ad dollar performance. But the more fundamental case, which requires relatively significant multi-year margin expansion, is hurt by the higher spending.

Basically, the case for NMAX based on incremental margins from ad and affiliate fee dollars only works if those dollars start going back to shareholders (and/or into the company's bank account). If they just are largely reinvested in salaries and production, bottom-line growth likely isn't strong enough for NMAX to grow into this valuation, even at the lows.

Betting On Newsmax Stock

Of course, what, exactly, this model looks like going forward isn't clear — even to Newsmax executives themselves. The company has been generating affiliate fees for less than two years. The impact of 2026 midterms and then the 2028 election spending isn't clear, but there is reason to suggest that both years (particularly 2028) will see a huge influx of political spending. In 2029, however, one wonders whether the departure of President Trump will lead to a more normalized, less eventful, news cycle and thus potentially lower viewership.

Again, these are at least normal questions to ask about a stock (as opposed to "How on Earth is Newsmax worth more than Fox?"). And at this point, it's not unreasonable to see Newsmax stock as a legitimate, fundamental buy. But personally, I'm not quite there yet — for two core reasons.

The first is that Newsmax is trying to grow into a secularly declining industry. And that decline is significant. Fox's subscriber base of 61 million dropped 9% in a year; that's an acceleration of a consistent long-term trend (the base is off 30% since 2018). That matters in terms of overall subs but also in terms of Newsmax's negotiating power with distributors; those distributors are not making much money off cable to begin with, and probably are fine letting Newsmax walk rather than giving up a not-immaterial portion of subscriber profit for a channel that doesn't lead in its own category.

The second, related, issue is that that management hasn't really proven itself. The way to offset the declines in linear media is to build out a digital business; the investments behind Newsmax 2 clearly show that Newsmax leadership at least understands that. The problem is that even experienced media executives have struggled in this new world, and to this point, Newsmax has had little success building out its digital business. The Digital segment generated just $17.5 million in revenue in the first half, and a chunk of that came from legacy businesses such as nutraceuticals and books.

Admittedly, there's a way to see that small size as yet another positive (presumably, there is much more revenue Newsmax can capture in that channel as well) but at the very least, it does require a good amount of faith in management to navigate a difficult environment against a massive, entrenched competitor. And as with Newsmax from a fundamental perspective, it's not unreasonable to bet on Newsmax to succeed — but nor is it yet a convincing bet, either.

data.title: Amentum Holdings: Near-Term Weakness Overwhelms The Long-Term Bull Case
data.description:
Investment action

I give a hold rating for Amentum Holdings (NYSE:AMTM) as I believe the long-term fundamentals remain strong, but near-term growth visibility is murky. AMTM is well aligned with key US defense priorities and positioned to capture secular tailwinds from nuclear modernization and energy transition themes. However, high leverage, a weak FY26 revenue outlook, and a softening funded backlog limit the scope for multiple expansion in the short term.

Company description

AMTM is a large-scale government services provider focused on advanced engineering, technology support, and R&D for the US government and its allies. It generates the vast majority of its revenue (~80%) from the US government, with the Department of Defense [DoD] being its largest end market at just over 50% of sales. AMTM was formed through a series of transactions, combining the services of businesses spun out of AECOM and Jacobs Solutions and the acquisition of PAE. It reports in two segments: Global Engineering Solutions [GES], which makes up about 60% of sales, and Digital Solutions [DS].

The Bright Spots

There are solid reasons to be optimistic about AMTM, particularly in how well positioned it is for US national security priorities. For instance, AMTM has strong alignment with the "Golden Dome" missile defense program, unmanned and counter-unmanned systems, efforts to reinforce the INDOPACOM posture, and border security. I know these sound like buzzwords, but they are not. These are generational programs that are backed by real funding. Recent legislation such as the "One Big Beautiful Bill," which includes $150 billion in additional defense spending, further shows the scale of this opportunity.

Regarding this Golden Dome program, my view is AMTM will be able to win the contract, and I say this because has a strong and long-standing, partnership with the Missile Defense Agency [MDA] through the IRES (Integrated Research & Development for Enterprise Solutions) contract, and I see this as a moat that competitors do not have. AMTM website has a higher-level information on what this IRES contract is but to put it simply, under IRES, AMTM provides the engineering and digital infrastructure needed to develop, test, and sustain the entire US Missile Defense System. This basically covers things like systems engineering, modeling and simulation, command and control integration. This is very important to note because this means that AMTM is already a key partner to the MDA for its defense systems, which also means it has in-depth knowledge on what has been built and how they integrate/operate with other parts of the system.

This relates to the Golden Dome program because it is effectively the deployment phase of some of the technologies and systems AMTM helped to design under IRES - things like sensors, digital twins (AMTM developed digital twins and ground-to-space architectures for MDA), and AI pattern-recognition algorithms (which is a key piece for Gold Dome to identify and respond to threats in real time. Source: Q3 2025 call). Viewed from another angle, this Golden Dome program is not entirely new, but are already integrating and using technologies that already exist, and given that the US government wants this Golden Dome program to be fully operational within 3 years, I would think that they will choose a vendor that they have worked with for years, one with working products and knowledge of the in-and-outs.

The US defense industry is competitive, with competitors such as Leidos, SAIC, and Northrop Grumman likely to submit proposals as well, but AMTM should have an edge here because of its operating history with MDA. These peers would need to convince MDA that they plan to build and integrate these systems will work, while AMTM is already multiple steps ahead. It has already demonstrated that it can do what MDA needs (integrating command, control, and sensor systems at operational scale). This little difference should be the winning step for AMTM since this is evidently an urgent and complex project. The way I see it, the path ahead is quite clear. The government needs to decide between onboarding a new partner that would need time to learn the architecture, or continuing with the incumbent that designed and sustains it. I believe AMTM represents the most credible and lowest-risk option.

My high-level view is that this alignment should translate into a healthy pipeline and tangible growth opportunities, which shields AMTM from the typical volatility/noise of defense budget negotiations.

My high-level view is that this alignment should translate into a healthy pipeline and tangible growth opportunities, which shields AMTM from the typical volatility/noise of defense budget negotiations.

Beyond AMTM equity story is not just about defense, another underappreciated growth lever is AMTM's "front-end" nuclear business. While this segment contributes only ~$700 million (source: Q3 2025 call), or ~5% of total sales, it is growing at a double-digit pace with positive incremental margins. In essence, this business handles the high-value work of designing, constructing, and operating nuclear power assets, including Small Modular Reactors [SMRs]. The demand outlook here is extremely positive given the surge in demand for reliable baseload power, driven by AI and data center builds. To put that in context, according to management's estimates, the addressable market for nuclear-related projects is expected to more than double over the next decade, which means the potential market size is ~$40 billion (currently $20 billion) (also from Q3 2025 call). AMTM's expertise across the entire nuclear lifecycle gives it a credible shot at capturing a meaningful share. If this segment continues to scale, I believe it could eventually force a re-rating of the entire company.

Finally, underlying demand indicators remain strong. AMTM's reported book-to-bill of 1x is good, but it doesn't tell the real story. When unconsolidated joint ventures are included, Q3 2025 book-to-bill jumps to 1.8x. That, combined with a $44.6 billion backlog and an additional $29 billion in pending awards (from Q3 10-Q filings and earnings call), provides solid revenue visibility and meaningfully de-risks the near-term outlook, I believe.

All in all, AMTM is well positioned not just to benefit from the current defense investment supercycle but also from energy transition themes.

Concerns

That said, there are several headwinds that temper the near-term outlook. The most immediate concern is the weak growth profile for FY26. To start, there are a few factors that will weigh heavily on reported revenue, including headwinds from recent divestitures, the de-consolidation of certain joint ventures following contract recompetes, and one fewer week in FY2025. On a standalone basis, they don't matter, but when combined, these items create a material headwind to top-line growth.

Funded backlog is also showing signs of softness, which contradicts with the bullish narrative I laid out above. If you read/hear the Q3 2025 earnings call, an important point was noted by one of the analysts (Noah from Goldman Sachs)-how does AMTM reconcile the opportunity with the funded backlog getting smaller? Management's response was that timing was the reason for that happening, and this could be true. But as a public investor that doesn't have access to the books, this softness is not a bullish sign. Funded backlog represents the most visible and near-term portion of revenue, so any weakness here could point to slower contract funding or conversion.

Up until here, the general concern I have for AMTM's P&L is the potential for very weak FY26 reported growth and further sentiment weakness if funded backlog doesn't tick up. The former would make the near-term fundamentals look unattractive, and the latter would cause investors to be even more conservative in the near-term growth, which I expect to weigh on the stock multiple.

On the balance sheet, leverage remains elevated. Although AMTM has made meaningful progress on paying down debt, net debt to EBITDA still stands at roughly 3.5x, well above peers' averages (Leidos Holdings, BAE Systems, Parsons, and Lockheed Martin have an average ratio of ~2.6x). This elevated debt position limits AMTM's ability to return capital, and instead it needs to focus on paying down debt (which also impacts how aggressively it can invest in the business to pursue growth).

Outside of the fundamentals, I am also worried about the technical. Private-equity sponsors Lindsay Goldberg and American Securities still own a significant stake in AMTM. On one hand, this suggests that these investors still think AMTM is undervalued, but it also means that at some point, they will sell, and that will limit the upside momentum in the stock.

Valuation

Despite me pointing out the bullish aspects of AMTM's equity story, I believe there are merits to the bear case (as I noted above) that will continue to weigh on the stock multiple until FY27 at the earliest. Currently, defense peers trade at mid- to high-teen forward EBITDA multiples, which is a big premium to AMTM's 8.4x. This discount seems fair to me given the weak FY26 growth outlook and soft-funded backlog (which is what investors use to anchor their near-to-mid-term growth expectations). Until we lap most of FY26 (when investors start to price in FY27 growth) and the funded backlog sees rejuvenated strength, I doubt the stock multiple will see any meaningful upwards revision.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for AMTM. The long-term story is well anchored by defense modernization programs, AI-driven energy demand, and a growing nuclear front-end business. However, near-term headwinds such as divestiture impacts, backlog softness, and elevated leverage will likely cap valuation until FY27. Once these overhangs clear, AMTM's visibility and profitability profile should improve, setting up a more attractive entry point. For now, I see risk and reward as balanced.

data.title: Clearwater Paper: Reiterating Buy Despite A Soft Q3 Guide
data.description:
We were neutral on Clearwater Paper (NYSE:CLW) given the absence of catalysts, limited growth, and elevated maintenance CAPEX, then upgraded to Buy in early July 2025 - an ill-timed call, as Q2 results followed and the stock fell by nearly a third (Fig. 1); today we return with a Q2 update and a reaffirmed positive view. As a reminder, our buy rating was supported by 1) Augusta's recent acquisition, 2) 100% USA-based industrial production, 3) a supportive product mix going forward with a margin upside, and 4) ongoing restructuring activities.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Q2 top-line sales reached $392 million thanks to the Augusta acquisition. The company also reported a Q2 adjusted EBITDA of $39.9 million. This was supported by volumes of ~305k tons, slightly ahead of the 304k ton estimate, while the average net selling price reached $1,182 per ton. Compared to Wall Street estimates, Clearwater's adjusted EBITDA and shipments aligned with guidance. Looking at the H1 results, Clearwater Paper reported net sales and EBITDA of $770 million and $70 million, respectively. These results were also at the midpoint of management's outlook range. The net leverage ratio was stable, and the company also repurchased additional shares.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, on a positive read-across within the North American industry, both International Paper and Smurfit WestRock are closing mills and shutting down industrial capacity. This is positive for the entire industry. Additionally, regarding Clearwater Paper, the EUR/USD exchange rate has become significantly less favorable for European paperboard producers. Indeed, these players will not be able to sell into the US market as the year progresses (Fig. 4). Additionally, with a 15% tariff announcement on EU imports into the US, we believe Clearwater Paper will likely gain market share and increase its margins.

The company is progressing well on share repurchase activity. Clearwater bought back 148.1k shares for $4 million during Q2 (Fig. 2). This is aligned with its $100 million share repurchase program announced in November 2024. Following a minus 35% since July 2025, we believe the company will increase its buyback activities.

Management is exploring a move into CUK (Coated Unbleached Kraft). It remains receptive to CRB (Coated Recycled Board) via acquisitions (Fig. 3). Clearwater is close to finishing a market and engineering review to enable CUK output on an existing SBS machine - effectively a mix shift with the ability to toggle between grades rather than a net capacity add. While investment CAPEX is not final, the team floated an indicative ~$50 million spend and an 18-month timeline, primarily driven by equipment lead times. This supports the margin product MIX and is consistent with our initial equity investment thesis. On CRB, management sees the most likely path as buying existing CRB capacity - or converting a suitable asset such as a recycled containerboard. We don't anticipate near-term news here, but given the industry's limited pool of attractive targets, this is a good moment to act quickly.

Clearwater noted roughly $40 million of remaining direct maintenance spend for the year alongside ongoing reinvestment in its assets. For Q3 2025, the company reported guidance of $10-20 million in Adjusted EBITDA (Fig. 5). Additionally, the company anticipates scheduled annual maintenance at the Lewiston mill. This will pressure fixed-cost absorption. For this reason, we are lowering our 2025 EBITDA estimates. That said, we are looking beyond these numbers. Clearwater Paper is able (with a mid-cycle operating rate) to achieve $1.85 billion in sales with a 13% EBITDA margin and a free cash flow conversion of 40-50%. Considering a $330 million debt position (adjusted for the cash), the company trades on a <2.5x EV/EBITDA. This is not the NTM EV/EBITDA, but the company's mid-cycle implied valuation.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Last time, we reported a forecast industrial capacity utilization of 80-85%, which we confirmed based on the latest company's assumption. While we see a modest change in sales, the Q3 2025 EBITDA outlook is below our internal estimate. The company guided $10-20 million, below our $30 million estimates. For this reason, we decided to lower our 2025 EBITDA; however, as reported above, the company continues to report fixed cost elimination for $40/50 million annually. As already noted, "we see an asymmetric setup for Clearwater Paper shares," due to a depressed multiple valuation and a M&A upside.

Even without applying a mid-cycle valuation, reverse engineering the 2025 EBITDA, and considering the H1 and Q3 companies' forecasts, we derive a yearly value of $120 million. Applying a 6.5x EV/EBITDA (aligned with its historical average and well below sector median - Fig. 6), the company's enterprise value reached $780 million. Deducting the current debt ($328 million), Clearwater Paper's equity value is estimated at $452 million. This represents a 48% upside from the current value. So, our target price is set at $28.2 per share.

Fig 6

Risks

Clearwater Paper downside risks include: 1) additional CAPEX requirements (and CAPEX inflation), 2) imbalance supply demand in North America region, 3) FX changes related to lower import from the EU, 4) no more tariffs, 5) changes in raw materials price, 6) energy price changes, 7) higher transportation costs and 8) client concentration risks.

Conclusion

Despite the disappointing Q3 guide, we continue to expect a combination of capacity rationalization to restore a better balance and lower net imports. Considering the potential upside on a stressed valuation and ongoing buyback, we reiterate our buy rating.

data.title: Packaging Corporation of America: Fundamentals Good, But Upside Limited
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Packaging Corp of America (NYSE:PKG) is a hold rating. In my opinion, PKG executes exceptionally well-outperforming the broader box market, winning share, and maintaining strong pricing power. Strategically, PKG is well positioned to benefit from industry-wide discipline as peers shift toward "value over volume," while the Greif acquisition provides more capacity. That said, with the stock already trading near my price target, I see limited near-term upside.

Business Overview

PKG is the third-largest producer of containerboard and corrugated packaging products in North America. The company's core business is making containerboard and corrugated packaging in its mills and plants across the United States. It also has a smaller paper segment that produces uncoated free sheet paper.

The Leader in a Changing Industry

My view is that PKG is the best-in-class operator in the North American containerboard industry. While it ranks as the third-largest player by capacity, I'd say PKG operates way better than the overall market. The evidence is in the pudding. Refer to the chart above, PKG has consistently outperformed the broader US box market (who are the peers of PKG), steadily taking share while larger peers have stayed flat. Look at the chart below; PKG shares went from 10% to 16%, while peers were largely flat outside of Westrock (which has been acquired by Smurfit Westrock PLC).

Even if we look at the latest data, in Q2 2025, PKG's corrugated product shipments per workday grew 1.7% y/y, while the overall industry shrunk by 1% y/y, leading to a 2.7% outperformance. I believe this consistent outperformance isn't due to luck. It points to real structural advantages, stemmed from a highly efficient asset base and a disciplined go-to-market [GTM] approach that prioritizes service and reliability.

For the former, PKG's efficiency stems from its long-term reinvestment strategy. Over the years, PKG has maintained capex at ~125% of depreciation (which means it is investing in the assets). This constant reinvestment has kept its mill system modern and reliable. This reliability, in turn, powers PKG's GTM model. What sets PKG apart here is not that it is the lowest-cost producer, but its ability to consistently command a price premium.

The reason is actually very strategic and smart. PKG deliberately targets smaller local and regional customers instead of chasing large national accounts. These customers tend to have customized product needs and depend heavily on PKG for services like logistics and warehousing. One of the key reasons that could really make this work is also that PKG focuses on building a strong localized sales force. You need boots on the ground that know the local preferences to find smaller customers, and this sales force is not something that can be replicated easily overnight.

Because PKG delivers on reliability and service, it has the right to charge more. Referencing Q2 2025 again, in just this quarter alone, higher prices and mix added $107 million to Packaging segment revenue (which is basically the entire y/y growth). This consistent reliability, then, helps PKG to build a deeply loyal customer base, creating a mindset for customers that asks, "If PKG serves me well and has been consistent, why should I risk going to another vendor if it will disrupt my operations?"

Competitor landscape shift

I believe the US containerboard industry is entering a period of structural change, and PKG is positioned to be the winner. In my opinion, PKG's two largest competitors are moving away from the long-standing "volume-at-all-costs" mindset and are now prioritizing "value over volume." In other words, what I expect is a more disciplined approach, one that will result in them walking away from low-margin or unprofitable contracts to protect profitability. This is a significant change vs. the past. As these players cut away less profitable accounts, PKG naturally becomes the destination for that "churned" volume. I strongly believe PKG has what it takes to take over this churned volume, without sacrificing pricing power, since it has proven its service levels and reliability. To be exact, PKG is unlikely to win over all the churned volume, but among them, there must be some customers that value services and reliability, and those will be the targets of PKG.

Corrugated box volumes were 3% lower on a same-day basis with Argentina being an outsized drag on the region's demand picture in the fourth quarter, along with our value over volume strategy seeing some pockets of volume contraction in places like Brazil and Colombia as we continue to roll through some legacy contract structures. Smurfit WestRock FY24 earnings call

Our go-to-market value over volume reset is essentially complete. We expect the final unfavorable impact to volume to be behind us later this year. IP Q4 2024 earnings call

This shift is also driving faster rationalization of inefficient capacity. What I meant here is that as peers lose volume (since they are cutting contracts), their internal need for containerboard declines, which means utilization rates of assets go down, and that makes it difficult to justify heavy capex spending on those aged mills. The result has been a steady wave of permanent mill closures across the industry, and this dynamic tightens overall supply. With demand typically growing by low single-digit percentages (refer to the first chart above), this lack of supply means better pricing power for the industry players.

The Greif Acquisition

PKG's recent $1.8 billion acquisition of Greif's containerboard business is a solid move. This move isn't about growing scale for the sake of it, but one that should materially strengthen PKG's competitive position. By acquiring Greif's assets, which include two containerboard mills with 800,000 tons of production capacity and eight corrugated plants, PKG effectively pulls forward the timeline for its capacity to come online. Relative to spending capex and building it from scratch, this acquisition allows PKG to immediately take advantage of the industry shift I mentioned above. Strategically, the implications are even better. The added footprint expands PKG's geographic reach and enhances its logistics network, allowing for shorter shipping distances and lower freight costs.

Valuation

I guess the unfortunate aspect of PKG is that the market seems to have priced in all the near-term upside. In my model, I expect PKG to sustain its high-single-digit revenue growth rate forward, supported by a better pricing environment, less competition intensity, and a broader footprint (from the Greif acquisition). I am not penciling in margins to go up, as this has been the historical range. With this, I think PKG deserves to continue trading at 18x forward PE since the closest peer-International Paper (IP)-trades at 17x with a low-single-digit growth outlook. But even at 18x, my price target is only $210, which is just about where PKG trades today.

Hence, because of the lack of upside, I am only giving PKG a hold rating for now.

Risk

An economic downturn will hit demand, which is already weak (2.6% fall industry-wide in Q2 2025). PKG is also undergoing a legal overhang (the July 2025 class-action lawsuit) which alleged PKG of industry-wide price-fixing. This creates uncertainty and could result in major penalties. There is also the case where PKG market share gains could also reverse if peers abandon their new "value over volume" strategies. Finally, volatile input costs could squeeze margins.

Conclusion

My view for PKG is a hold rating. To be fair, PKG checks nearly every box for solid fundamentals; it has kept up its assets, has a loyal customer base, and also benefits from a less competitive environment. However, with much of this strength already reflected in the valuation, I believe the risk-reward is not attractive. PKG remains a high-quality business worth keeping on the radar, but at current levels, I prefer to wait for a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Airbnb: Q3 2025 Performance May Disappoint As Growth Normalizes
data.description:
In this article, I shall discuss the performance metrics that investors may want to monitor in Airbnb's (NASDAQ:ABNB) upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call in November 06, 2025, attributed to the mixed travel indicators heading into the quarter and the "weaker demand growth for alternative accommodations."

Pending further growth catalysts, I believe that the stock may continue underperforming due to the premium valuations and the mixed trading volumes.

ABNB's Meltdown May Continue For A Little Longer, Pending Growth Catalysts

ABNB 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, ABNB has indeed retraced by -14.7% compared to the wider market at +39.2%, with it apparent that the stock has fallen victim to its previously premium valuations and the intensifying competition in the alternative accommodations as more players enter the fray, including Booking (BKNG) and Expedia (EXPE).

Even so, despite the narrowing moat, it is apparent that the management's decision to double down on the Services/ Experiences segment has been able to trigger the relatively decent growth profile in the FQ2'25 performance metrics across:

  • Gross Booking Value [GBV] of $23.5B (+10.8% YoY) compared to a year ago at +10.9% YoY,
  • Nights and Seats Booked at 134.4M (+7.4% YoY)/ +8.6% YoY,
  • Revenue at $3.1B (+12.7% YoY)/ +10% YoY,
  • adj EBITDA at $1B (+11.8% YoY)/ +9.1% YoY, and
  • EPS at $0.98 (+19.5% YoY)/ -12.7% YoY, respectively.

Demand for ABNB's differentiated, alternative accommodations and experiences remains robust as well, as observed in the growing average Daily Rate [ADR] at $174 globally (+2.3% YoY).

This is compared to a year ago at +2.4% YoY along with the US hotel averages of $162.17 in Q2'25 (based on April, May, June 2025 numbers), albeit paling in comparison to EXPE's notably higher ADR at $209 (inline YoY).

ABNB's headwinds also stem from the lower demand in the North American segment, which currently comprises 30% of the company's total Nights and Seats Booked, barring which, the company may have reported "double digits" growth in FQ2'25.

ABNB Valuations

Thanks to the stock price underperformance, ABNB has been growing into its FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 14.93x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.95x, down from the 5Y mean of 58.41x/ 54.16x and the 1Y mean of 17.12x/ 30.98x, respectively.

Even so, when compared to the sector EV/ EBITDA median valuations of 10.36x and the sector P/E non-GAAP median valuations of 16.99x along with its travel peers, including:

  • Trip.com Group Limited (TCOM) at 18.25x/ 19.12x,
  • EXPE at 8.43x/ 14.78x, and
  • BKNG at 17.45x/ 23.21x, respectively,

it is apparent that ABNB remains relatively expensive at current levels after the recent recovery from April 2025 bottom of 13.55x/ 24.83x.

Part of the pessimism may be attributed to the mixed US travel spending trends in September 2025, where the US Travel Association reports that "hotel demand remained soft, down 0.4% year-over-year, while short-term rental demand grew" as "overseas inbound travel continued to lag, falling 2.9% in August—the fourth straight month of declines hovering around the 3% rate."

A similar trend has been observed in Wedbush’s travel data, where there has been a "weaker demand growth for alternative accommodations (-2% in August yoy from +10% in Q2)," worsened by "the international trends for alternative lodging suggests the implied results are trending below management’s guidance."

With ABNB set to report their FQ3'25 earnings call in November 06, 2025, readers may want to closely monitor their performance against the prior FQ3'25 guidance of:

  • revenues of $4.06B (+30.9% QoQ/ +9.7% YoY) against a year ago at +10% YoY,
  • Nights and Seats Booked at +7.4% YoY/ +8% YoY, and
  • adj EBITDA of $2B (+200% QoQ/ inline YoY)/ +7% YoY, respectively.

It goes without saying that the decelerating FQ3'25 growth on a YoY basis has been discouraging worsened by the mixed market indicators above, with the implication of a potential miss against the management's guidance already triggering the stock's recent underperformance and the loss of its prior Q2'25 price gains.

If anything, a similar pullback has been observed in the cruise sector as well, with Royal Caribbean Group (RCL), Carnival Corporation (CCL), Viking Holdings (VIK), and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) already reversing in momentum after the double digit gains from the April 2025 bottom.

This is despite the durable consumer appetite for cruises during the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the ongoing tariff war, as similarly observed in CCL's double beat FQ3'25 performance, the higher customer deposits, and the raised FY2025 guidance.

As a result of the reversing market sentiments surrounding the travel sector despite the Fed's recent rate cut, I believe that ABNB may need to deliver outsized performance in the upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call to prevent a further stock price meltdown beyond the -27.4% correction from 52 week highs and -45.1% from the February 2021 peaks.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is especially since the consensus have been consistently lowering their forward estimates, with ABNB expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +9.7%/ +10.9% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +11%/ +15.7% a year ago and the 5Y historical top-line growth at +18.2%, with it exemplifying its maturing growth profile after the double-digits pandemic-induced growth and the painful normalization in its future prospects moving forward.

The one silver lining to ABNB's investment thesis will be its extremely healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $9.36B (+1.1% YoY), thanks to the rich LTM Free Cash Flow generation at $4.27B (inline sequentially).

This is on top of the prudent shareholder return through the retirement of -3.1% of its over the LTM and -8.4% since FQ2'22, as its EPS growth decelerates.

ABNB Stock Remains A Hold Here

ABNB 5Y Stock Price

For now, these mixed reasons have already contributed to ABNB's rather underwhelming 5Y chart, with the still expensive valuations and the decelerating growth profile implying further near-term corrections, pending the materialization of positive catalysts and/ or outsized FQ3'25 performance.

My caution is attributed to the stock trading beyond my base-case fair value estimates of $102.50, based on the April 2025 P/E bottom of 24.83x (still expensive compared to the peer group mean of ~21x) and the LTM EPS performance of $4.13.

Based on the consensus FY2027 EPS estimates of $5.60, there appears to be a minimal upside potential of +16.9% to my long-term price target of $139 as well.

ABNB's Lack Of Bullish Support

The inherent lack of bullish support has been observed in ABNB's technical indicators as well, despite the numerous oversold levels since late 2023 and the mixed trading volumes, with the lack of a trading floor presenting risks to anyone looking to buy the dip.

As a result of the possible capital losses and the uncertain FQ3'25 performance arising from the tightened discretionary/ travel spending as the tariff risks kick in, I prefer to maintain my Hold (Neutral) rating for the ABNB stock here.

Metrics That May Change My Mind

The few things that may convince us to change my current Hold rating will be if:

  • ABNB manages to command a higher premium and higher ADR for its alternative accommodations/ value-added services, allowing them to eventually drive higher GBV and richer margins,
  • domestic travel trends within the North American region improve, allowing ABNB to drive accelerated recovery in its performance metrics,
  • ABNB can expand its supply and active listings to drive growth through the higher nights booked, to balance the intensifying competition headwinds, and
  • ABNB can offer more value added services to deliver one-stop shop travel booking experience, similar to other Online Travel Agencies like BKNG and EXPE, where travelers can book flights/ accommodations/ rent cars/ transportation/ admission tickets and thus, drive higher bookings and grow mindshare.

Pending these tailwinds, it may be more prudent to observe from the sidelines in the mean time.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/17/25
data.description:
2This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

data.title: Upexi: Offering Registration Likely Limits Upside (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in May, I initiated coverage of Upexi, Inc (NASDAQ:UPXI) and called the stock a 'sell' due to its inflated valuation relative to the combo of its legacy business and its Solana (SOL-USD) themed DAT strategy. Since that 'sell' call, UPXI shares have fallen by as much as 70%. Currently, UPXI shares are just 40% off that mid-May sell call:

Given the fact that SOL is actually up 5% over that same stretch, I think it would be fair to call UPXI's performance as a SOL-proxy considerably poor over these last 5 months. As I said at the end of that May piece, good assets can be purchased at a bad price. And even though Upexi paid a great price for its SOL earlier this year, $10 for UPXI in May was a bad deal. At a price currently under $6, prospects look a lot better.

Solana Strategy and mNAV

Just as (Micro)Strategy (MSTR) had been able to do for the last several years, Upexi quickly scaled up its Solana holdings through various financial instruments like common stock and convertible note issuance. According to data tracked by Blockworks Research, Solana DATs hold roughly 15.8 million SOL combined.

Of that combined SOL position through DAT companies, Upexi holds a little over 2 million SOL and is the fourth largest Solana-themed DAT in the market. The table below breaks down the value of Upexi's Solana relative to its current market capitalization and its post-share registration diluted market capitalization:

mNAV Calculation Prior To Offering

Offering Completion
Shares 58,888,756 94,457,980
UPXI Price $5.61 $5.61
Solana 2,018,419 2,018,419
Solana Price $185 $185
Solana Value Per Share $6.34 $3.95
mNAV 0.88 1.42

Source: Upexi, Analyst's Calculation, $185 SOL price at article submission

Utilizing a basic calculation alone, UPXI arguably looks cheap relative to its SOL holdings based on a $5.61 closing price and a $185 SOL price on October 16th. Things get a bit more murky due to the recent offering registration that could see as many as 48 million shares sold from buyers of Upexi's PIPE and convertible note. The problem for UPXI shareholders is the proceeds from the offered shares go to the selling stockholders rather than the company.

Thus, this would be expansion of the float that wouldn't lead to SOL purchases for UPXI shareholders. Not included in that offering is roughly 5 million in additional shares through options, warrants, and other instruments:

Excluded From Share Count UPXI Shares
Options ($3.39 ex) 621,353
Warrants ($4.20 ex) 1,848,735
Debt Converts 186,667
Preferred Converts 138,889
RSU from Incentive Plan 2,250,000
Total 5,045,644

Source: Upexi

Given those shares, we can calculate a fully diluted mNAV for UPXI at closer to 1.5. The justification for a fully diluted mNAV premium this large is debatable, in my view. The company has the ability to raise capital through its $500 million equity line. Raising capital in this way makes sense in a world where mNAV premium is extended. But I suspect as long as the stock trades at a discount to basic mNAV, diluting existing shareholders through that $500 million equity line is less likely. Especially considering such a large cohort of shareholders have registered their shares for sale, including CEO Allan Marshall - though he would retain the majority of his equity.

As I see it, Upexi's immediate path to acquiring more SOL is likely limited to its staking strategy.

In Upexi's last earnings report, the company noted an 8% annualized yield from staking SOL. This yield has come down quite a bit since the end of Upexi's last reported quarter. Investors can think of the relation between staked assets and staking yields similarly to the way they would bonds or dividends; the more demand there is for yield, the lower the yield goes.

Upexi's Staked SOL 2,018,419
Annual Yield as of 10/16 6.5%
Annualized Return at $185 $24,271,488

Source: Analyst's calculations

The good news is even at the current annualized SOL staking yield of 6.5%, 2 million staked SOL at a value of $185 per coin generates over $24 million in staking rewards. This would actually flip Upexi to a profitable company, all else being equal.

Legacy Business

It's important to remember that even though the company's equity valuation is now largely tied to the daily performance of Solana, Upexi continues to operate its legacy consumer product business. Even with roughly $1 million in digital asset revenue during the year ended June, Upexi still saw top line revenue decline year over year by 43% and lost $13.7 million during the fiscal year:

$9.8 million of Upexi's $11.2 million decline in revenue year over year was due to its divesting of its re-commerce business. Still, this implies that even adjusting for the re-commerce portion, Upexi's owned-brand consumer product business looks to have contributed roughly $1.4 million to that year over year revenue decline. The point is, if Upexi continues to operate its consumer business, it could come at the expense of SOL holdings in the future either through liquidated staking rewards or capital raised through equity offerings going toward opex.

Closing Thoughts

As I see it, the potential headwind of 48 million shares coming into the float without leading to direct SOL purchases limits the upside of the stock. At a basic mNAV discount, Upexi theoretically has an incentive to sell Solana and buy back common shares if doing so could generate greater returns than the staking yield from the SOL. But I largely view such a strategy as unlikely. My expectation is UPXI will perform in correlation with SOL for the foreseeable future, but may continue to lag the underlying in the event large quantities of the registered shares are sold. I'll cautiously upgrade UPXI to a 'hold' due to the possibility that basic mNAV could re-rate from a discount to a premium in the event SOL surges in price. But I still don't personally have any interest in buying shares of UPXI at this point in time.

data.title: SEIV: Deep Value, Differentiated Sector Mix
data.description:
With low valuations and a balanced market cap exposure, the SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value Factor ETF (BATS:SEIV) is a value-oriented fund with a twist: a sector exposure that overweights technology and communication services, unlike most funds in the category. Still a relatively new fund, the combination of attractive valuation and strong returns warrants investors' attention. The coming sections will assess SEIV’s portfolio composition, valuation, and performance, stacking it up against the broader market and value peers.

ETF Description & Highlights

SEIV aims for long-term total returns through the investment in U.S. companies trading below their fair value, according to SEI Investments’ methodology. The fund adopts a fundamental approach, which consists of a rules-based stock selection framework that screens companies using factors like earnings, cash flow, sales, and typical valuation multiples, including price-to-earnings. The fund may also incorporate into this process additional metrics such as momentum, quality, and low volatility.

As of October 9, 2025, SEIV had nearly $887 million in assets under management, invested across 117 companies, with an average market cap of $68.4 billion. While the fund’s name highlights its large-cap focus, in reality, its portfolio looks more balanced across the capitalization spectrum than traditional benchmarks, like the Russell 1000. As such, SEIV has a 39.3% spread across large and mega caps, with mid caps accounting for 37.5% and small caps for 22.1%. By comparison, the Russell 1000 has 43.2% in mega caps and 32.2% in large caps, leaving less than 35% for mid and small caps.

SEIV’s top ten holdings are Nvidia (3.58%), Microsoft (3.40%), Apple (3.17%), Alphabet C (2.77%), Hewlett Packard Enterprise (2.68%), Alphabet A (2.49%), AT&T (2.37%), Micron (2.27%), Verizon (2.17%), and Cisco (2.16%), which together represent 27% of total assets. This list underscores a strong tilt toward technology and communication services, where names like HPE, Cisco, Micron, AT&T, and Verizon are all trading below 17x P/E.

The following table brings a mix of value-oriented funds with a broad exposure to the equity market. This list will serve as the peer group for SEIV in the analysis. The first two, the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the iShares Core S&P U.S. Value ETF (IUSV), are “classic benchmark” members of the group. Both follow cap-weighted indexes, Russell 1000 Value and S&P U.S. Value, respectively, and hold diversified portfolios of large- and mid-cap value U.S. stocks. Next, the Dimensional U.S. Marketwide Value ETF (DFUV) is actively managed and rooted in an optimization model that focuses on book-to-market and profitability factors. Meanwhile, the Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF (VFVA) uses a rules-based, actively managed strategy that screens for U.S. companies trading below intrinsic value and with a small- and micro-cap tilt. The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV) emphasizes shareholder yield, blending dividends and buybacks as parameters to identify value. Finally, the WisdomTree U.S. AI Enhanced Value Fund (AIVL) adopts a machine-learning model to select stocks that appear undervalued.

SEIV’s largest sector is technology, accounting for 30.8% of the fund, followed by financial services (18.2%), communication services (14.4%), and healthcare (11.7%). Smaller weights include consumer discretionary (10.6%) and consumer staples (5.1%), while minor allocations are found in industrials (3.3%), utilities (3.3%), energy (1.6%), basic materials (0.6%), and real estate (0.4%). Relative to the Russell 1000, SEIV is overweight in financial services (+5.1%), communication services (+4.4%), and healthcare (+2.5%), offset by an underweight allocation to industrials (-5.1%) and technology (-3.7%).

The fund's stance on technology is quite significant for a value-oriented fund, with about 20 holdings spread across several segments. In hardware & storage, the fund owns Apple, HPE, NetApp, and Dell Technologies, making it the largest technology segment, unlike most funds, where semiconductors and software make up the bulk of the sector. Yet, holdings in Nvidia, Micron, and Qualcomm make semiconductors account for about 7% of the whole fund, while software remains with 4% through holdings in Microsoft, Zoom Video, and Five9.

In financial services, the fund has a meaningful exposure to insurance players like AIG, Unum, and Prudential, and also to reinsurance companies such as RenaissanceRe, AXIS Capital, and Assured Guaranty. Regional banks are also significant for the fund, with shares of Citizens Financial, Popular, Zions, and Bank OZK, while Synchrony Financial, Ally Financial, and OneMain add further exposure to the consumer finance segment.

The fund’s third largest sector, communication services, is focused on two pillars. The first is Alphabet, with more than 5% weight. The other one includes telecommunication service providers AT&T and Verizon, alongside broadband and media provider Comcast.

Outside the top three sectors, SEIV’s most meaningful positions include Cardinal Health and McKesson (drug distribution), Pfizer (big-cap pharma), Gilead Sciences (biotechnology), General Motors and Ford (auto industry), Delta Air Lines, United Airlines, American Airlines (airlines), Kroger (grocery), Exelon and FirstEnergy (regulated utilities), as well as APA, Valero, and Marathon Petroleum (energy exposure). Together, these names add a defensive sleeve to the fund with significant positioning in mature and relatively stable operations compared to the overall market.

SEIV’s heavy allocation to technology and communication services represents a distinct sector mix compared to value-oriented peers. This leads to overweight positions of 16.1% and 8.6% relative to the group. On the flip side, the fund is underweight in virtually all sectors, particularly industrials (-8.8%) and energy (-6.2%), areas in which peers rely on these segments to bring value to investors.

As a result, SEIV has a moderate sector correlation with most funds in the 0.48 to 0.62 range. The exception is IUSV, which has a relatively high correlation of 0.88 with SEIV, as both share a large exposure to technology and maintain a modest stance in industrials.

Attractive Valuation

At an 11.1 P/E, SEIV trades at a steep 50% discount to the Russell 1000’s 22.4. This, of course, is consistent with the fund’s goal of holding an undervalued portfolio. The valuation gap is apparent in a sector-by-sector comparison with the benchmark, as all sectors trade at a discount. This makes the fund’s significant position in technology, a high-multiple sector by nature, not a detractor to its overall value profile, as some of the fund’s core holdings in the sector, like Hewlett Packard Enterprise, Dell Technologies, Micron, and Qualcomm, trade at particularly attractive valuations, all below 17x P/E.

But the fund lags in growth, especially given its historical earnings growth of 3.7% which is nearly half of the Russell 1000’s 7.4%, driven by the fund’s defensive profile in segments like technology, healthcare, and financial services, as highlighted by holdings in Micron (3Y EPS growth 1.1%) and AIG (-5.3%), for instance.

With an EBITDA margin of 25.7% and an ROE of 46.9%, profitability measures are lower than the benchmark by nearly 10 percentage points. Weaker margins are spread across most sectors, including technology, financial services, the biggest detractors, as well as healthcare, industrials, and consumer-related sectors, since many companies trading at a discount also exhibit lower margins than core holdings of the broader equity indexes, such as big techs or names like Visa, Mastercard, Eli Lilly, and AbbVie.

SEIV trades at the low end of the trading range compared to peers, along with VFVA (10.5x P/E) and WTV (12.2x). Valuations of these two funds seem justified, in my view, given their modest stance in technology and a large allocation to smaller companies: VFVA (55%) and WTV (32%), which usually trade at a discount to the rest of the market. On the flip side, P/E multiples of IWD, IUSV, and AIVL sit above 16%, still in the value camp, but at a significant premium to other peers, primarily due to their large exposure to mid- and large-cap companies relative. In summary, despite a heavy allocation to technology, SEIV still looks cheap, driven by a value approach across all sectors, including technology, and a relatively diversified allocation to all market sizes, making it one of those so-called deep-value funds in the category.

Outperforming Peers

With inception on May 18, 2022, SEIV has recently completed three years of activity. During this period, its annual return of 22.3% slightly trailed the Russell 1000’s 23.2%, as broader equity indexes have benefited from the performance of big techs, particularly in 2023 and 2024. Still, SEIV’s performance has been quite strong compared to other value funds, with the highest return in the 3-year period, and it has consistently stayed within the top 3 funds in each of the three years.

SEIV’s volatility has been marginally above the Russell 1000, with a 3-year beta of 1.03 and capture ratios indicating a modest tendency to amplify downside market moves. To be sure, that is not the norm among value funds, with four out of six funds exhibiting more contained volatility than the benchmark. VFVA and WTV also show above-average volatility, due to their exposure to small caps, which are typically more volatile than their large-cap counterparts. In summary, while most value peers usually see more moderate price action during declines, the trade-off is that SEIV has outperformed in rallies, as these more conservative peers have shown upside capture ratios lower than 100, which helps explain SEIV’s outperformance during a period that has been predominantly bullish for stocks.

On trading activity, SEIV’s daily average volume of $4.33 million is well below large funds like IWD and IUSV, but remains ahead of VFVA and AIVL. However, its bid-ask spread (0.16%) is the second widest, which is a disadvantage for short-term trades, especially if compared to most liquid funds with spreads below 0.04%. The fund’s turnover of 32% is below the peer group average, but still higher than IWD and DFUV, while its expense ratio (0.15%) is in line with peers, which I would say looks reasonable for an actively managed fund. It also offers a dividend yield of 1.53%, above the broader market average of about 1.10%, but slightly lower than peers, while its tracking error in the 5% to 6% range is the lowest in the group. Finally, the fund’s 3-year Sharpe ratio of 1.20 is by far the highest among peers, reflecting its outperformance despite a higher volatility.

Final Thoughts

SEIV’s structure reflects an attempt to capture undervalued opportunities across the market capitalization spectrum. And its tilt toward technology, financials, and communication services sets it apart from value peers, typically more concentrated in defensive sectors. However, it has also contributed to the fund’s higher volatility. Despite the sector mix, SEIV trades at roughly half of the Russell 1000’s valuation, leaving it in deep-value territory. The flip side is moderate earnings growth that may limit the upside going forward.

In sum, my view is that SEIV’s attractive valuation and diversified sector mix look well-suited for a market that seems constructive overall, but shows mixed economic signals, while trade tensions continue to affect short-term sentiment. On top of that, the fund’s sector allocation makes it an option for investors targeting value, but seeking to diversify away from conventional sector allocations tilted to defensive sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, and energy.

data.title: Home Buyers' Strike Drags On Relentlessly, Despite Lower Mortgage Rates
data.description:
So mortgage rates are not the problem; they’re at the low end of the pre-QE historical range. Those exploded prices are.

Despite slightly lower mortgage rates, demand for homes by homebuyers who need a mortgage dropped further in the latest reporting week, the third weekly decline of purchase mortgage applications in a row, according to data from the Mortgage Bankers Association on Wednesday. Compared to the same week in 2019, purchase mortgage applications were down by 34%.

Demand in the housing market plunged three years ago, after home prices had exploded over the preceding two-year period of a mega-FOMO-driven frenzy, fueled by mortgage rates the Fed had recklessly repressed via QE to way below the rate of inflation. So mortgage rates are not the problem; those exploded prices are.

These applications for mortgages to purchase a home are a measure of demand that may become actual home sales in the future and are therefore an indicator of future home sales. And they remain dismal.

The average weekly mortgage rate for conforming 30-year fixed mortgages declined a hair to 6.42%, according to the MBA on Wednesday. This measure bottomed out last year in September and early October 2024 at 6.13%, just before the Fed started cutting interest rates.

Last year, from mid-September through mid-December, the Fed cut its policy rates three times, by a total of 100 basis points, despite re-accelerating inflation. The bond market, including the mortgage market, got spooked about a lax Fed in light of re-accelerating inflation and went the opposite way. From mid-September 2024 through early January 2025, mortgage rates jumped by 100 basis points.

After that episode, the Fed talked hawkish and put rate cuts on hold to keep a lid on the bond market. Even now, as rate cuts have started again, the Fed is talking about “careful” rate cuts and being “concerned” about the “rising inflation” and “watching it closely,” etc., etc., while reacting to labor-market weakness. The last thing they want is another spike in long-term yields.

The Fed’s reckless policy of pushing mortgage rates far below the rate of inflation via QE, including the purchase of mortgage-backed securities, pushed mortgage rates below 3% even as inflation was raging toward 9%, thereby creating the most negative “real” (inflation-adjusted) mortgage rates ever.

For homebuyers, the steeply negative real mortgage rates were better than free money, and when money is free, prices don’t matter, and homebuyers went nuts. During this period I called it the Most Reckless Fed Ever. And as we now know, its recklessness has done enormous long-lasting damage to the housing market.

A funny thing happened with refis. That small drop in mortgage rates in mid-September by just a few basis points apparently pierced a magic line, and a bunch of homeowners suddenly refinanced mortgages, causing a spike in refi applications, but it lasted only for two weeks, and then it was over again.

Refi applications edged down further in the latest reporting week, after having plunged over the prior two weeks from the two-week-wonder spike.

It seems a bunch of homeowners had been sitting on the edge of their chairs waiting for rates to inch down to a magic level, and then they pounced, and there were no more people left sitting on the edge of their chairs? It’s funny because mortgage rates barely moved over those six weeks.

The previous sudden spike in demand for refi mortgages occurred a year ago in September 2024, when the MBA’s measure of the average weekly 30-year fixed mortgage rate dropped as low as 6.13%, before surging again:

Refi mortgage applications (red in the chart below) and mortgage interest rates (blue) are in an inverse relationship. The below-3% mortgage rates while inflation was raging toward 9% caused not only an explosion of home prices but also a historic refinance boom when a huge number of mortgages were refinanced.

That spike of refi mortgage applications a few weeks ago was small compared to the boom years of negative real mortgage rates, but exceeded the volume in 2017, 2018, and early 2019.

This refi boom in 2020-2021 caused a large portion of mortgages to carry very low interest rates, and homeowners now cling to these low-interest-rate mortgages as if they were valuable assets. Nevertheless, life happens, and homeowners are gradually getting out of these mortgages and loosening the phenomenon of the “lock-in effect.”

The share of below-3% mortgages outstanding declined in Q2 to 20.4% of all mortgages outstanding, the smallest share since Q2 2021 (red in the chart below). And the share of 3%-3.99% mortgages declined to 32.1%, the smallest share since Q3 2019 (blue).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nestle: Strategy Update Is A Sign Of Change (Upgrade)
data.description:
After five years that the stock has been essentially dead money with very little growth, declining margins, and two CEO transitions, Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) seems to finally turn a new leaf.

The newly elected CEO, Philipp Navratil, seems to be actually willing to do what it takes to inject energy into sleepy old Nestlé, with no better evidence than his willingness to say the 'L' word, for layoffs.

With Nestlé set to cut nearly 6% of its workforce, I argue this is the sign of change investors have waited for, and upgrade Nestlé to a 'Buy'.

Sometimes, It's All About Chance, As Philipp Navratil Is The Accidental CEO Nestlé Needed

From 1981 to 2024, Nestlé had four CEOs, with an average term of over 10 years. Since 2024, Nestlé already replaced its CEO twice.

Phillipp Navratil, the current CEO, was with the company for a long time and last served as the CEO of the Nespresso division. He was promoted to the CEO role after the former CEO, Laurent Freixe, was dismissed after he was found to have a romantic relationship with an employee.

Personal issues aside, I argue that this is a great development for Nestlé shareholders. I had a lot of criticism for Freixe's plans, which I viewed as lacking teeth, or as I called it, a "Let’s Do Good Things & Stop Doing Bad Things” plan.

In my last article, I wrote that:

If they were really ambitious with cost savings, we would have seen significant layoffs by now.

Well, it's finally happening.

New CEO's Plan Signals Real Transformation

One month into his role, the presentation from Phillipp Navratil tells me that this guy has been thinking about how he would fix Nestlé for quite some time. His plan includes four pillars:

The first two pillars are things the previous CEO talked about as well, and they are turnaround 101 in large-scale consumer companies. In short, they want to focus on growing products that meet consumer demand where it is, aggressively pursue growth areas, and ruthlessly cut underperforming lines.

This is easy to say, hard to do, and it requires a certain personality. With the previous CEO, I was more skeptical, but the new CEO gives reason to be optimistic, because of pillars 3 and 4.

A company like Nestlé, that's been around for a long time and has 277,000 employees, can get complacent. Many employees are old timers; there's a lot of bureaucracy, and too many people who give their input.

In situations like these, individual performance tends to be left behind, while seniority gets prioritized. Well, the new CEO is saying, not under his watch. He wants to create a performance culture, with performance-based compensation and clear KPIs.

Lastly, the cherry on top, the new CEO is showing he means business, by targeting a 6% workforce reduction, laying off 16,000 employees. He also plans to create a more agile organization, which should lead to faster decision-making, more efficient operations, and accountability.

Nestlé's Results Are Also Showing Signs Of Improvement

Nestlé only discloses sales figures on a quarterly basis, whereas full financials are reported on a semiannual basis. The sales figures were quite strong in Q3 and marked a meaningful improvement.

Real internal growth, which is a metric that measures mix and volume, was up 1.5% in the quarter, the highest level in quite some time. Meanwhile, pricing was up 2.8%, leading to a total organic growth of 4.3%, a post-pandemic record.

All zones had positive RIGs and positive organic growth, while all product segments except for Confectionery had positive RIG, and all of them had positive organic growth.

Nestlé also shared the big difference between its winning and losing SKUs, whereby the priority growth products have grown 14% in the first nine months of 2025, while the underperforming lines were flat.

This goes to emphasize the opportunity that a cold-hearted decision maker can capitalize on if they execute properly.

Looking ahead, they have maintained the medium-term guidance for now, but increased their cost savings plan to CHF 3.0 billion, up from CHF 2.5 billion. As they progress, I believe more aggressive targets could be delivered.

Valuation

In previous bearish articles, I argued that Nestlé is facing structural issues that are hard to overcome. I wrote:

Simply put, companies that don't grow revenues and earnings have very few avenues to keep up with the market. And companies that trade at relatively high valuations ahead of a three-year stagnation are very likely to not even keep up with bonds.

That is, in a nutshell, the story of Nestlé of the past three years and why I remain bearish, even after the appointment of a new CEO.

I just think that the company's issues are structural, and unless a very aggressive CEO comes in and takes almost a private-equity-like approach to a turnaround, I continue to believe the stock will provide poor returns.

Well, at CHF 82 per share, Nestlé trades below 19 times forward earnings. Translating to ADR, shares will probably open around $102, which would reflect 18 times forward earnings and 17 times next year's estimates.

This is no longer a high valuation. In fact, it's a low point for Nestlé historically, and it's about in line with other consumer staple peers like PepsiCo (PEP) and Unilever (UL).

Meanwhile, Nestlé is now an arrow pointing up story. Growth is accelerating, and margins are likely to improve under the new CEO.

As such, I think they could recover to at least a 20x multiple, reflecting a target price of $115 in the near term.

Conclusion

There was never a question about Nestlé's wide moat and strong brands. The concern was a lack of growth and an unwillingness to do what's needed to reignite the company's earnings growth story.

I believe the new CEO has the right plan, and my impression is that he's the man for the job. As numbers already begin to improve, I upgrade Nestlé to 'Buy'.
